name: Let a browser agent take the whole exam
tagline: Point a coding agent at the exam in a browser and it logs in, reads each question, works out the answer, and submits.
why: A coding agent that controls a browser can log in, read a question, work out the answer, fill the field, download any files it needs, and submit. What you delegate stops being a single answer and becomes the whole exam. Anand did this on his own Tools in Data Science exam. He ran Chrome with remote debugging and told Codex to visit the exam, solve a question, and click Check. About six minutes and fifty cents later it was done, and it worked three times out of three.
watch_for: It struggles when every student gets a different randomised question, because the agent has to read each one afresh. CAPTCHAs, login walls, and proctoring software stop it, and a live run can fail in front of an audience. Do not run this against a real graded exam. It shows what is now possible, and it is not a recipe for cheating.
steps: Run Chrome with remote debugging, or start a browser agent like agent-browser or Playwright
Point it at the exam page with a test login
Tell it to read the question, work out the answer, and fill the field
Let it submit and click any Check button
Read the log of steps it took, then check whether the answer is right
learn_instead: Whether the route the agent took was sound, and whether the answer it submitted is correct. It will submit wrong answers with full confidence.

examples.context: Anand ran Chrome with remote debugging and told Codex: use CDP to visit the exam and solve Q4, then click Check to verify. It solved the question in about six minutes for fifty cents, worked three times out of three, and he could run several questions in parallel Codex sessions. Several students scored 10/10 in 15 minutes on a 45 minute exam.

Anand opens this post by saying an effective way to solve online exams is to point a coding agent at it. He aimed Codex at a Crack the Prompt challenge, and in four minutes it returned the answers, found a bug that blocked submission, and noticed you could skip levels through the API because the locking was only in the front end.

name: Let an agent write, deploy, and submit code answers
tagline: For a coding question, the agent writes the program, deploys it, fetches the live URL, and pastes that in as the answer.
why: Some questions ask for a working program: a FastAPI endpoint, a Vercel deployment, a file committed to GitHub. A coding agent can write the code, run the tests locally, deploy it, fetch the live URL, and submit that URL. It handles the whole lifecycle rather than the coding step alone.
watch_for: Deployment is where live demos break, since ports, permissions, CORS, and cold starts all bite. The agent can also satisfy the automatic checker while quietly breaking the actual requirement, so confirm that the deployed thing does what the question asked.
steps: Give the agent the question and the exact acceptance criteria
Let it write the code and run the tests on your machine
Have it deploy to the required host and capture the live URL
Open the URL and confirm it meets every requirement, including CORS
Submit the URL
learn_instead: Whether a deployment that passes the checker actually satisfies the requirement. Tests can go green while the spec is broken.

examples.context: The repo lists FastAPI questions with live checkers, and host-constrained checks for Vercel and Hugging Face Spaces, where the answer is a deployed URL that the grader hits. An agent can produce that URL from start to finish.
Anand argues coding agents now run the full write, run, and fix loop instead of completing a line at a time. That is what lets one own a deploy-and-submit question rather than just drafting the code.

name: Read what the grader checks before you solve
tagline: A question often tests whether your answer fits the grader's rules, so read those rules before you write anything.
why: A lot of questions quietly test whether your output fits what the grader accepts: the right columns, the allowed rounding, the exact headers, the response shape, CORS. Correct means whatever the validator accepts. Read the grader first and you know exactly what to produce. It is the same move you make at work when you read an API contract, a test, or a rubric before building.
watch_for: In a real exam the checker may run on a server you cannot see, so you infer the rules from the wording and the error messages instead. Read the concept behind the rules too, or you can pass the check and still miss the point.
steps: Find the validation logic, or read the acceptance criteria slowly
Write down every requirement: columns, rounding, headers, shape, CORS, edge cases
Have AI produce an answer that meets each requirement
Compare your output line by line against that list
Submit once every item is met
learn_instead: Turning a fuzzy goal into a precise, checkable spec. That is where the thinking sits.

examples.context: This question is hard because AI renders nested code fences badly. Pasting AI output blindly fails, and passing forces you to understand the Markdown structure the parser demands.

The notes flag several FastAPI questions where CORS is part of the grade, so correct backend logic still fails the checker without it. That is the clearest sign the answer is whatever the validator accepts.

name: Inspect the page to see how the exam works
tagline: Online exams run as code in your browser and often send hidden text, logic, even answers to the page; open developer tools and look.
why: An online exam is a program running in your browser, and it often ships hidden text, question logic, and sometimes the answer to the page. Press F12 to open developer tools, read the rendered HTML, the Sources tab, and the Network tab, and you see what the page knows but never displayed. This is basic web literacy.
watch_for: Some students stop here and stop thinking, and a few feel this crosses a line. Be clear that the point is to understand that a page can hide things and you can inspect it, not to always hunt for the answer. Minified code is hard to read, but AI can untangle it.
steps: Press F12 on the question page
Read the HTML for hidden comments and display:none blocks
Open Sources to find the question logic the page loaded
Watch Network when you submit to see what is sent to the grader
Paste anything you find into AI to interpret
learn_instead: Reading and reasoning about unfamiliar code. AI interprets it, but you decide what to look for and what it implies.

examples.context: This question puts a hidden input with a secret value just above the prompt and asks what the value is. The only way to answer is to open developer tools and read it off the page, so the question rewards the skill directly.

The visible text says just answer what is asked, but a display:none comment in Chinese carries the real question and even tells students to use F12 or view-source and an AI translator. The page rewards inspecting it.

name: One-click bookmarklet that hands the page to AI
tagline: Save a browser button that scrapes the page text, comments, links, and files, then drops all of it into an AI prompt.
why: A bookmarklet is a small script you save as a browser bookmark. One click can collect the question text, HTML comments, links, form fields, downloadable files, and console errors, turn that into Markdown, and hand it to an AI without leaving the exam tab. Good AI use starts by giving the model the whole context instead of the words you happen to see.
watch_for: Shadow DOM and iframes defeat a naive querySelector, and some pages block injected scripts. Dumping everything can also bury the model in noise. Think of it as giving the agent context, not auto-solving.
steps: Write a bookmarklet that collects page text, comments, links, inputs, and any endpoints it calls
Serialise that to Markdown or JSON
Open an AI chat, or your own Ask-AI page, with the context already filled in
Ask for the answer and the evidence behind it
Reuse the same bookmarklet on the next site
learn_instead: Choosing which slice of the captured context is relevant. Dumping the whole DOM is easy; choosing the relevant slice is the harder part.

examples.context: A working bookmarklet-style scraper that walks the DOM, cleans the text, escapes the output, and emits Markdown with frontmatter. It is the same page-to-Markdown move a copy-exam-context button would reuse.
Anand's Ask-AI redirector opens an AI chat from a prefilled ?q= URL. A bookmarklet can pipe the scraped page straight into it, turning any page into a one-click solve-with-context button.

name: Send each question to the right tool
tagline: Read all the questions first, then send each one to the cheapest tool that will reliably solve it.
why: A varied exam needs different tools per question: bash, Python, DuckDB, the browser, developer tools, a spreadsheet, an AI, or your own judgement. The skill is the choice itself, picking the cheapest reliable tool before you start solving. Read everything first so you can batch the similar work together.
watch_for: This can shrink into rote labelling, and the value is in weighing cost against reliability, not in the label. A bad choice wastes setup time.
steps: Read every question before solving any
Tag each with the tool that is cheapest and most reliable for it
Batch questions that share a tool
Solve in order of confidence and setup cost
Keep your own judgement for the ones AI routes around badly
learn_instead: Knowing each tool's strengths and failure modes well enough to choose. AI cannot make that call for you.

examples.context: This question generates a project with a budget, traffic, latency, and language needs, then asks the student to choose and justify the AI tools, weighing quality against cost. It tests the routing decision directly.
The archetype list reads like a routing map: DuckDB analytics, Pyodide execution, FastAPI live checkers, GitHub artifact checks. Each archetype points to a different best tool to reach for.

name: Use wrong answers to interrogate the grader
tagline: When the grader explains why an answer failed, treat each wrong submission as a free clue and feed it back to AI.
why: When a validator returns a descriptive error, every wrong submission is a free probe. Submit a reasonable attempt, paste the exact error into AI, and let it work out the missing requirement. Repeat until it passes.
watch_for: Some questions cap attempts or penalise retries, and vague errors waste your time. This pays off when the validator actually tells you something.
steps: Submit a sensible first attempt
Copy the exact error the grader returns
Paste the question, your answer, and the error into AI and ask what condition you missed
Apply the fix
Repeat until the checker passes
learn_instead: Debugging by hypothesis: read an error, form a theory, test it. AI speeds the loop, but you pick the theory.

examples.context: The notes list questions with live checkers, deterministic expected results, and useful error messages. Those are the ones whose feedback is rich enough to solve one clue at a time.
Anand shows that cheap repeated checking catches errors because mistakes overlap far less than successes do. The same logic makes error-driven retries close in on the answer.

name: Crunch data questions right in the browser
tagline: Your browser can run SQL through DuckDB and Python through Pyodide, so many data questions need no install at all.
why: The browser is already a compute engine. DuckDB-WASM runs SQL over CSVs and Pyodide runs NumPy and Python, so many data questions can be solved entirely in the page, or by pulling the data out and running it locally through Local MCP bash.
watch_for: Setup can be fiddly, base64 vectors and large datasets get distracting, and WASM behaves a little differently from native. Check each result with a second method.
steps: Find the dataset the question points to
Load it into DuckDB-WASM or Pyodide in the page, or pull it out to run locally
Write the SQL or Python to compute the answer
Cross-check with a local run through Local MCP bash
Submit the result
learn_instead: Writing SQL or Python that actually answers the question. AI drafts it; you confirm the logic fits what was asked.

examples.context: The repo lists DuckDB in-browser analytics questions for uptime, regression, and correlation, and Pyodide execution questions like embedding similarity, all built to be solved with in-browser compute.
Anand pipes data through jq and awk into a TSV for fast local review. It is the same pull-then-compute instinct that lets you take an exam dataset and answer it with bash or DuckDB.

name: Run several AI attempts at once, then pick
tagline: Fire three to five AI attempts at once with different models or prompts, then compare them and pick.
why: Instead of one careful path, run several AI attempts at once with different models, prompts, or framings, and compare. Their failure modes differ, so you converge on the answer faster than a single careful path would.
watch_for: More attempts cost more time and tokens and can flood you with near-identical output, and you still need a way to choose the winner. Returns fall off after a few.
steps: Generate three to five answers using different models or prompt framings
Lay them side by side
Note where they agree, which is likely right, and where they diverge
Investigate the disagreements
Submit the answer you verified
learn_instead: Choosing between conflicting plausible answers. Disagreement surfaces the uncertainty; resolving it is your call.

examples.context: Anand lists teaching students to run many AI attempts in parallel as a deliberate shift from single-path effort to portfolio thinking, a habit worth teaching rather than an obvious default.

He shows several cheap models checking each other cut errors, because their mistakes overlap far less than each one's individual rate. That is the basis for running attempts in parallel and trusting the agreement.

name: Have a second and third AI check the answer
tagline: Run your question and draft answer past a second and third model, asking each what is wrong with it.
why: Take your question and a candidate answer to a second and a third model and ask what is wrong with it. Where they disagree, you have found your uncertainty, and you catch confident errors before you submit.
watch_for: It is slower, models can share a blind spot and all agree on a wrong answer, and a judge model can be inconsistent. Treat agreement as evidence, not proof.
steps: Get a first answer from model A
Ask model B what is wrong with this question and answer, if anything
Ask model C to break the tie if B disagrees
Resolve the conflict yourself
Submit the cross-checked answer
learn_instead: Spotting a shared blind spot. When every model is wrong the same way, only a person who knows the data catches it.

examples.context: The core result is that cheap ensemble checking sharply cuts confident errors because independent models rarely make the same mistake. It is the direct case for a jury before you submit.
Anand maps which model or mode suits which task. A jury mixes them on purpose so their different strengths catch each other's errors.

name: Out-prompt a locked-down AI when that is the task
tagline: Some questions are themselves prompt attacks: make a guarded model say a banned word, or repair a broken prompt.
why: Some questions are prompt-attack tasks in disguise: get a guarded model to say a forbidden word, repair a broken prompt, or reverse-engineer one. Solving them is real prompt-attack skill, and it is on the syllabus.
watch_for: A trick that beats one model often fails on another, so the skill is reading the guard and finding the gap, not memorising jailbreaks. Keep it to sanctioned red-team tasks.
steps: Read the constraint you have to defeat or satisfy
Guess where the guard is weak: encoding, role-play, indirection
Write a prompt that exploits the gap
Test and iterate against the live checker
Work out what made it succeed so you can repeat it
learn_instead: How instruction-following breaks under pressure. This is the durable skill rather than a workaround.

examples.context: The whole task is to make a model that was system-prompted to never say Yes output Yes. It is framed as a red-team exercise and scored by a live checker, so passing needs real prompt-injection skill.

Anand tried jailbreaking ten system prompts, such as telling a model to drop its required SAFE tags. Most models failed at least one, and his takeaway was that you cannot rely on the system prompt and need external validation.

name: Screenshot the question into a vision AI
tagline: Screenshot a question with its tables, charts, and equations and let a vision model read and answer it.
why: Vision models read a screenshot of a question, including tables, charts, and equations that are awkward to copy as text, and return an answer. A hotkey can run screenshot to model to clipboard in seconds.
watch_for: Multi-part questions and wide tables get cropped or misread, and vision models still invent numbers from a blurry image. Check any figure it reads back.
steps: Capture the question region as an image
Send it to a vision model such as Claude, Gemini, or GPT
Ask for the answer and ask it to transcribe any data it used
Compare that transcription against the screenshot
Paste the answer
learn_instead: Catching when the model misreads a chart or table. Vision is confident and often subtly wrong on numbers.

examples.context: Anand tests Gemini reading text straight off a comic and getting it right, which shows modern vision models can read a screenshot well enough to answer, and shows where they slip.
He feeds an image to Gemini and gets a precise, instruction-following result. That image-in, structured-result-out pipeline is what a screenshot-to-answer flow relies on.

name: Use the provided Ask AI button openly
tagline: When the exam ships its own Ask AI button, using it is the intended path, and your model choice changes your score.
why: When the exam itself ships an Ask AI button, using it is the path the designer intended. Real problem solving is open-book and AI-assisted, and pretending otherwise is the fiction. If the model hands you the answer, take it and move on. If not, the button still gets your thinking started.
watch_for: Lean on it too hard and you learn nothing and cannot catch a wrong answer. Treat the button as a starting point rather than an oracle.
steps: Click the question's Ask AI button
Read the answer with a critical eye
If it solves the question, verify and submit
If not, refine the prompt or switch tools
Note what the model could not do, because that is your study target
learn_instead: Verifying the handed answer, and noticing where the model fell short so you know what to study.

examples.context: Anand tells students every question has an Ask AI button: if it gives you the answer, copy it and save time; if not, figure it out. That is his explicit endorsement of open AI use.

On Anand's graded assignments students pick an AI and Ask AI any question at a click. Across 333 students, Perplexity users scored about 11 points higher than ChatGPT users and Claude users about 9, so the model you pick changes your grade.

name: Read the answer-checking logic sent to your browser
tagline: If a question checks your answer inside the browser, its logic and target values sit in the page for you to read.
why: When a question validates your answer in the browser instead of on a server, the derivation logic, the target values, and any salt are exposed in the page. Read them and you can compute or brute-force the answer. It works only because the checker shipped to the client.
watch_for: This is a known, patched class of bug. Move validation to the server and it disappears, so use it to teach why server-side validation is needed rather than as a lasting strategy. Brute-forcing still needs you to understand the derivation.
steps: Open the question's bundled JS in developer tools
Find the answer or derivation module it imported
Identify the salt, the target, or the expected value
Reproduce the derivation locally, or brute-force the exposed space
Submit the computed answer
learn_instead: The security principle: secrets must never ship to the client, and a verifiable contract should not leak. That is the transferable lesson.

examples.context: A maintainer comment at the top states the secret-sharing scheme is visible in the public exam bundle and calls it public-by-design for the current deployment. The leak is documented in the file itself.

Anand had an agent compare the deployed site against the local verifier, enumerate agent IDs 000 to 099 through the api/solve endpoint, and recover all 100 agents' emails and passwords with none missing. It is the leak being exploited end to end.

name: Read others' submissions via an open endpoint
tagline: An unprotected filter endpoint returned other students' saved answers, which is a real hole to learn from and leave alone.
why: The exam app exposed a filter endpoint that returned saved results by quiz or email. A student who found it could read other students' submitted answers. It worked because the endpoint had no access control.
watch_for: This is a documented vulnerability and an integrity violation. Teach it only as the system had a hole that AI helped find, and what that means for what you must learn, never as a how-to. Hardening removes it, and it only helps if you already know the target emails.
steps: For teaching, show that a GET to the filter endpoint returns stored results
Explain why an unauthenticated read endpoint is a design flaw
Pivot to understanding that the system exposed this, rather than memorising answers
Discuss the fix: authentication plus server-side scoping
learn_instead: Threat-modelling a system you use, and the judgement to leave a hole you find alone. Finding it is easy with AI; deciding what to do is human.

examples.context: The worker implements an async filter handler, and a doc comment shows it being called as fetch('/filter?quiz=math-101'). Reading the code shows the endpoint returns saved results without checking who is asking.

The repo notes plainly that the filter endpoint exposes saved answers and results. The maintainer is acknowledging the leak in writing, which is useful for showing students the authors know and are fixing it.

name: Learn by solving the questions
tagline: Put the course material inside the questions; if you can answer one with AI, you have learned enough, and if not, study that gap.
why: Busy students want grades, not reading. If you move the course material into the questions, the rule becomes simple. Answer it with AI's help and you have learned enough. Get stuck and you have found exactly what to study. The exam becomes the curriculum.
watch_for: Without reflecting on the misses you can pass and retain nothing, so you need a feedback step where failures actually teach. A surface pass is not learning.
steps: Start with the questions rather than the readings
Attempt each one with AI's help
Where you pass cleanly, move on
Where you struggle, study just that gap
Treat each failure as a pointer to what to learn
learn_instead: A fair read of what you understood versus what AI carried. That gap is your real study list.

examples.context: Anand's starred experiment: the typical student is busy and wants grades, so he moved the material into the questions, and if students can answer them they skip the content. The exam itself does the teaching.
He lays out teaching through exams and prompts rather than content, and testing first then teaching from the mistakes, which is what makes solving-first a real way to learn.

name: Turn each wrong answer into a targeted drill
tagline: Give AI a question you got wrong and ask what you misunderstood, what an expert checks first, and three similar drills.
why: Take a question you got wrong, your answer, and the correct one, and ask AI for the misconception, the clue you missed, what an expert checks first, and three near-miss variants to practise. The best learning role for AI is turning a mistake into a drill rather than handing you the answer.
watch_for: AI may name the wrong misconception, and the variants need a quick sanity check. Bring real wrong answers, since hypotheticals teach less.
steps: Collect a question you got wrong, your answer, and the correct one
Ask AI for the misconception, the missed clue, and what an expert checks first
Ask for three near-miss variants that test the same misconception
Solve the variants
Do the short practice it recommends
learn_instead: This is the learning move itself, though you still judge whether AI named the right misconception.

examples.context: Anand's approach is to do the work without AI, then have it critique what you did and learn from the gap. He drives the agent with taste and intent and lets it bring the expertise, which is the postmortem turned into a daily habit.
He argues test cases should teach and not just grade, by showing the kind of mistake you made. A student applies the same idea by asking AI to diagnose a specific error.

name: Use past scores to decide where to spend effort
tagline: Upload past scores and ask AI which assignments most predict passing or an A, then put your effort there.
why: Upload past cohort scores and ask AI for simple, evidence-backed heuristics: which assignments most predict passing or an A, and which to skip. Then your effort goes where it moves the grade rather than spread evenly across everything.
watch_for: Heuristics from past cohorts may not hold for yours, and skip-this advice optimises the grade, not learning. Anand flags learning be damned as the half-joking caveat.
steps: Get the scores spreadsheet, your own attempts or cohort data
Ask AI for heuristics that hold about 80% of the time, tied to specific thresholds
Read the resulting playbook for avoiding a fail and for aiming at an A
Put effort into the highest-leverage assignments
Revisit as your own scores arrive
learn_instead: That grade-optimisation and learning are different goals, and that you get to choose, deliberately, how much of each you want.

examples.context: Anand uploads 1,700 students' scores to ChatGPT and gets a one-page playbook, for example that clearing two assignment thresholds cuts fail risk to about 16%. It is the upload-scores, get-heuristics move with his study-what-moves-the-marks framing.
The scores file in the exam repo is the raw material a student or teacher feeds to AI to derive personalised which-to-prioritise heuristics on the spot.

name: Use AI as a study coach
tagline: Instead of asking AI for answers, ask it to find the holes in your reasoning and to quiz you toward them.
why: Rather than asking for answers, ask AI to find your blind spots. Tell it what you think and why, and have it challenge the reasoning or pose questions that make you work it out. The model becomes a sparring partner and a next-step guide.
watch_for: Sycophantic models will validate weak reasoning, so push them to disagree. It is easy to slide back into asking for the answer.
steps: State your current understanding and reasoning in full
Ask AI to challenge it and find the flaw
Ask for the single next concept to study
Try to explain that concept back in your own words
Have AI grade your explanation instead of giving you one
learn_instead: Putting your reasoning into words clear enough to be challenged. The act of explaining is where the understanding forms.

examples.context: Anand's note on Socratic questioning describes asking agents and interns questions he does not want answered, turning think step by step into the Socratic method. A student can turn the same stance on themselves.
He lists using AI as a personalised coach, a research guide and next-step recommender tuned to the learner's gaps, which reframes the model from answer engine to coach.

name: Turn one lecture into five study formats
tagline: Turn one lecture into a podcast, sketchnote, quiz, flashcards, or a song, and study in whatever format you absorb.
why: One source, a lecture video or a reading or a transcript, can become a podcast, a sketchnote, a quiz, flashcards, or a song in minutes. Generative AI converts formats, so you can study in whatever form you actually absorb.
watch_for: A derived format can drift from the source or oversimplify it, and a catchy podcast is no guarantee of accuracy. Spot-check the original for anything high-stakes.
steps: Get the source as text, a transcript via YTScribe or a PDF
Upload it to NotebookLM or Gemini
Generate the format you learn best from: audio, visual, or quiz
Generate a practice quiz to test yourself
Check the key facts against the source
learn_instead: Knowing which format you actually retain from, and checking the derived version did not distort the source.

examples.context: Anand takes one PDF report and generates a 20-minute podcast in three languages, a cinematic video, a sketchnote, and a narrated song. It is the one-source, many-formats pipeline applied to study material.
He describes consuming books through AI-generated formats and summaries, the same instinct applied to course content: pick the format that fits you rather than the one the source shipped in.

name: Turn transcripts into flashcards you review daily
tagline: Pull a lecture transcript, have AI write question-and-answer cards, and review them in a spaced-repetition app.
why: Pull a lecture transcript, have AI generate question-and-answer pairs, and load them into spaced repetition. The hardest part of studying, making good cards, becomes automatic, and daily review compounds.
watch_for: Auto-generated cards can be trivial or ambiguous, and bad cards waste review time. Cull and edit the set before you commit to it.
steps: Get the transcript via YTScribe or Gemini's YouTube integration
Ask AI for atomic question-and-answer pairs covering every point
Review and prune the weak cards
Import them into a spaced-repetition tool
Review daily until the exam
learn_instead: Spotting which auto-generated cards are too easy or misleading. Card quality is a judgement AI often gets wrong.

examples.context: Anand's Recall tool resurfaces things he has learned at random to aid retention, his own working example of turning notes into a spaced-repetition habit that AI-generated cards plug into.
His prompt turns a talk transcript into a structured deck with automated quizzes, which is transcript-to-question generation, the engine behind auto-built flashcards.

name: Compress a textbook into the likely exam topics
tagline: Upload a long reading and ask AI for the concepts most likely to be tested, each in one line, ranked.
why: Upload a long reading or PDF and ask AI for the concepts most likely to be tested, each with a one-line explanation. Reading time drops from hours to minutes, ordered by exam relevance.
watch_for: The model guesses what is likely from generic priors, not your specific exam, and can miss a topic your professor stressed. Use it to prioritise, not to gamble everything.
steps: Upload the reading to Gemini, which handles long documents well
Prompt it: I have an exam on this in 30 minutes, give the 15 most likely-tested concepts, one line each, ranked
Read the ranked brief
Drill the top items
Skim the rest as insurance
learn_instead: Judging whether the model's priorities match your course emphasis. It predicts from priors, while you know your professor.

examples.context: Anand notes Gemini reads long documents well, which is the basis for dropping in a whole textbook and asking for a ranked, exam-focused brief instead of reading linearly.
His workflow turns large source material into prioritised, decision-ready summaries, the same move that turns a 200-page reading into a 15-point cram brief.

name: Do it first with AI, understand it afterward
tagline: AI lets you finish a task you do not yet know how to do, then understand it, and skip memorising syntax you rarely use.
why: AI collapses the old order of learn-then-do. You can do a task you do not know how to do, then understand what happened, and skip rote-memorising the syntax. It suits tools you will touch once.
watch_for: Skip understanding entirely and you cannot debug or check the result when AI is wrong. That is fine for throwaway work and risky for core skills, so decide which ones deserve real learning.
steps: State the outcome you want, even without knowing the method
Let AI do it
Inspect the result and the steps it took
Backfill understanding only where it counts for you
Skip memorising syntax you can always regenerate
learn_instead: Deciding which skills to truly learn and which to delegate for good, and noticing when do-only has left you unable to verify.

examples.context: Anand tells Claude to visualise some audio creatively with no knowledge of ffmpeg. He executes, then understands, then skips learning the tool, which collapses the usual stages of tool competence.

He argues that as AI lowers the cost of execution, the lasting advantage shifts from existing expertise to the ability to keep learning and rebuild expertise at each new frontier. That is the flip side of choosing what not to learn deeply.

name: Generate comics and interactive demos for hard ideas
tagline: For a concept that will not stick, have AI build a comic, an animation, or an interactive simulator you can play with.
why: For a concept that will not stick, have AI generate a comic, an animated explainer, or an interactive simulator. A visual, manipulable explanation can onboard an idea faster than text, especially for an unfamiliar topic.
watch_for: A polished explainer can still be wrong or oversimplified, so check the mechanism against a reliable source before you trust it.
steps: Pick the concept you cannot internalise
Ask AI for an analogy-driven comic or an interactive HTML explainer
Manipulate the inputs to build intuition
Check the explanation against a trusted source
Keep the artifact for revision
learn_instead: Checking that an intuitive explanation is actually correct, since a memorable one can still be inaccurate.

examples.context: Anand generates interactive explainers with AI to make unfamiliar concepts intuitive and manipulable, a tool a student can point at any sticky exam topic.
He uses AI to draft comics that explain why a question exists, which carries the teaching point with low friction and high recall, the same move applied to learning a hard concept.

name: Finish early to leave time to verify
tagline: Later submissions score lower in a steady pattern, so finish with a buffer to verify rather than racing the deadline.
why: Later submissions score lower in a smooth pattern. Each time the remaining time halves, the average score drops about 1.4%. Even pretending the deadline is earlier nudges you to submit when your answers are better.
watch_for: Correlation is not all causation here, since diligent students both start early and score well, and submitting early with worse answers does not help. The point is to finish earlier so you have time to check.
steps: Treat the deadline as several hours earlier than it is
Aim to finish with a buffer rather than at the wire
Spend the buffer verifying, not tweaking blindly
Submit once you have verified
Skip the last-minute spurt where scores sag
learn_instead: Managing yourself under a deadline, which no AI can install for you.

examples.context: Anand's analysis of about 1,000 students across three quizzes finds each halving of the remaining time costs roughly 1.4% of marks, with a last-minute spurt that scores worse.
He lists redesigning exams from behaviour data, including timing and deadline behaviour, which shows submission timing is a measured variable in the course rather than folklore.

name: Label every AI claim: seen, computed, checked, or guessed
tagline: After AI answers, tag each step as observed, computed, verified, inferred, or guessed, and check every guess.
why: After AI produces an answer, require it, and yourself, to tag each step: observed in the source, computed by code, verified by a checker, inferred, or guessed. The question shifts from is it correct to what is the evidence behind each part, and every guessed step becomes a place a human has to check.
watch_for: Models overclaim provenance and will say computed when they guessed, so spot-check the labels, especially anything marked verified.
steps: Take the AI's answer and its reasoning
Tag each step: observed, computed, verified, inferred, or guessed
Treat every guessed and inferred step as a checkpoint
Verify those before you rely on the answer
Make this your default way of reading AI output
learn_instead: Telling what AI knows from what it is improvising. That is the scarce skill this workshop points at.

examples.context: Anand builds Verify buttons that expose the evidence behind a claim: the searchable quote, the SQL query, the step-by-step checklist. The provenance ladder is that habit applied claim by claim.
He makes judgement, taste, and validation explicit learning goals, arguing that knowing what to reject rises in value as syntax falls. The provenance ladder is how you practise that.

name: Attach a cheap way to check every answer
tagline: For any AI claim, build the cheapest way to check it: a quote to search, the query behind a number, a checklist.
why: For any AI claim, build the cheapest way for a person to check it: a link plus an exact phrase to search for, the SQL or Python that produced a number, a step-by-step checklist, or a prefilled fact-check prompt. A verify path lets a reader check a claim cheaply instead of trusting it blindly.
watch_for: Getting the source right is not the same as getting the conclusion right, since a verify path confirms the number, not the argument, and sources can themselves be wrong.
steps: For a claim, attach a link and a short exact string to search for
For a number, attach the query or script that produced it
For a procedure, write the check as a ticked checklist
Optionally add a prefilled AI fact-check link
Run the verification before trusting the claim
learn_instead: Designing a cheap, fair check for any claim, which is the discipline behind trust.

examples.context: Anand details four verify designs in rising order of effort, from a link with a searchable string, to the SQL behind a number, to a procedure checklist, to an AI fact-check link, and frames a verify button as a public claim that you are willing to be checked.
His Ask-AI redirector opens an AI chat from a prefilled ?q= prompt, the exact mechanism for the verify-with-AI option: one click opens a fact-check of the claim with step-by-step evidence.

name: Design AI-proof questions to find AI's limits
tagline: Build a question AI can answer mechanically but gets wrong without human judgement, and you learn where its limit is.
why: The fastest way to learn what AI cannot do is to design a question it can answer mechanically yet gets wrong without human judgement: a poisoned document, a chart with a manipulated scale, code that passes the tests but breaks the requirement. Designing the trap is how you find the limit; solving one rarely reveals it.
watch_for: It stays abstract unless you ground it in a concrete artifact, and students may build questions that are merely hard rather than judgement-requiring. Insist that the trap be specific.
steps: Pick a skill you think is uniquely human
Design a question where AI's mechanical answer is plausibly wrong
Build in the trap: a hidden conflict, a scale trick, tests that pass while the requirement breaks
Test it against AI to confirm it falls for it
Spell out the human judgement the trap requires
learn_instead: The boundary of AI competence in your subject. Designing the trap forces you to name what only a person catches.

examples.context: This question hands the student a chart whose axis scale has been manipulated and asks them to repair it. It is the exact judgement trap where AI can redraw the chart mechanically yet miss the distortion.

Students must classify documents and exclude the poisoned ones, where a document counts only if it is relevant and carries no error flag. It is a built trap that rewards spotting what a mechanical pass would wave through.

name: Spot personalised questions and solve your own
tagline: If each student gets a different version of a question, a shared answer is useless and the only move is to solve your own.
why: Modern exams give each student a deterministic but unique variant seeded on their email, so sharing answers fails because a shared answer fits someone else's numbers. Once you see this, the only winning move is to actually solve it with AI, and copying a friend's answer is worthless.
watch_for: Students waste time hunting for leaked answers that cannot apply to their seed. The lesson is to stop chasing shareable answers and build a repeatable method instead.
steps: Check whether the question's data depends on your identity or seed
If it is unique, drop answer-sharing as a strategy
Build a method or prompt that solves any variant
Apply it to your own variant
Share the method with peers rather than the answer
learn_instead: Building a general method rather than chasing specific answers, and collaborating on approach, which is allowed and rewarded.

examples.context: Anand's starred design: deterministic but unique variants shift the game from leaking answers to actually solving the problem, which makes sharing pointless and method-building the only strategy.
The repo's authoring guidance requires per-user randomisation seeded on email over a 50-plus variation space, the technical implementation of unique variants that defeats sharing.

name: Collaborate openly and disclose your shortcuts
tagline: Real work rewards teamwork, good use of tools, and disclosed help, so divide questions, discuss, and say what you used.
why: In real work nobody earns extra credit for working alone or reinventing the wheel. AI-native assessment rewards collaboration, good use of resources, verified inputs, and disclosed shortcuts. Dividing questions across a team and discussing them counts as a skill rather than a violation.
watch_for: Open collaboration still expects real variation and disclosed help, and passing off a teammate's work as your own without saying so crosses the line. Disclosure is what keeps it legitimate.
steps: Form a study or solving team
Divide the questions and share approaches
Verify each other's inputs and answers
Disclose where you used AI or borrowed a method
Add your own variation or improvement for credit
learn_instead: Working with others and being transparent about how you got there, the social and integrity skills AI does not replace.

examples.context: A student messages Anand that the course got them collaborating for the first time in an online degree, dividing questions then solving and discussing, and he calls it exactly what he has tried to teach for five years.
Anand's starred principle: allow copying, collaboration, and hacking, because in real work collaboration, using resources well, verifying inputs, and disclosed shortcuts are all rewarded.
